title: Define a trait
instruction:
A trait declares method signatures. Types implement them.

Run this.
successMessage: Both Person and Robot implement Greet. They each provide their own hello(). Same interface, different behaviour.

title: Default methods
instruction:
Trait methods can have default implementations that types can override.

Run this.
successMessage: preview() has a default implementation using title(). Article gets it for free. Override only when the default is wrong for your type.

title: Trait bounds
instruction:
Use traits as constraints on generics: `fn foo<T: Trait>(x: T)`

Run this.
successMessage: T: Display means 'T must implement the Display trait'. The function works for any type that can be printed.

title: Multiple trait bounds
instruction:
Require multiple traits with `+`:

```rust
fn foo<T: Display + Debug + Clone>(x: T)
```

Run this.
successMessage: + combines trait bounds. T must implement both Display and Debug. The integer 42 satisfies both; so does &str.

title: Implement Display
instruction:
Implement `std::fmt::Display` to control how your type prints with `{}`.

Run this.
successMessage: Implementing Display unlocks println!("{}", ...) and format!("{}", ...) for your type.

title: where clause
instruction:
For complex bounds, use a `where` clause for readability:

Run this.
successMessage: where clauses move complex bounds out of the function signature, making both more readable.

title: Trait objects with dyn
instruction:
`dyn Trait` is a runtime-dispatched trait object. Use when you need a collection of different types.

Run this.
successMessage: dyn Animal is a trait object — runtime polymorphism. Box<dyn Animal> stores any Animal on the heap. The concrete type is erased, the trait interface remains.

title: Derive common traits
instruction:
Use `#[derive]` to auto-implement common traits.

Run this.
successMessage: #[derive] generates trait implementations for free. Debug for {:?}, Clone for .clone(), PartialEq for ==. Essential for most structs.
